note: Net migration figures come from ONS Long-term international migration (year ending December 2025, provisional, published 21 May 2026). Visa-grant figures come from Home Office Immigration system statistics (year ending December 2025). The two sources are not interchangeable: ONS counts people staying 12 months or more; the Home Office counts visas granted, which is always higher. ONS reason splits are for non-EU+ arrivals (inflow), not net migration by reason.

metrics.source_name: ONS, Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025
metrics.notes: Provisional. Nearly halved from the year ending December 2024; lowest since early 2021.

metrics.metric_name: Net migration (previous year, revised, for trend)
metrics.source_name: ONS, Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025
metrics.notes: Originally published as 431,000 (May 2025), revised to about 345,000 (November 2025) and to 331,000 here, largely due to higher British emigration estimates.

metrics.source_name: ONS, Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025
metrics.notes: Provisional. Down 20% from the updated year ending December 2024 (1,012,000); down from a peak of 1,469,000 in the year ending March 2023. Non-EU+ accounted for 627,000 (77%).

metrics.source_name: ONS, Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025
metrics.notes: Provisional. Down from the updated year ending December 2024 (680,000).

metrics.source_name: ONS, Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025
metrics.notes: 47% of non-EU+ immigration; the largest single reason. Main applicants 277,000; dependants 17,000.

metrics.source_name: ONS, Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025
metrics.notes: 23% of non-EU+ immigration; down 47% from 272,000 the previous year. Main applicants 71,000; dependants 74,000. The main driver of the fall in net migration.

metrics.source_name: ONS, Long-term international migration, provisional: year ending December 2025
metrics.notes: 14% of non-EU+ immigration; includes those who claimed asylum after regular arrival and via illegal entry. Family about 7%, humanitarian about 6% of non-EU+ immigration.

metrics.source_name: Home Office, Immigration system statistics, year ending December 2025 — Why do people come to the UK? Study
metrics.notes: Main applicants 406,824 plus dependants 19,647. Up 3% on the previous year; 35% below the year ending June 2023 peak (652,072). Dependants down 87% from peak after January 2024 restrictions.

metrics.metric_name: Work visas granted (total route, including dependants)
metrics.source_name: Home Office, Immigration system statistics, year ending December 2025 — How many people come to the UK each year?
metrics.notes: Main applicants plus dependants. Main applicants alone were 168,471, down 19% on the previous year and 50% below the year ending December 2023 peak.

metrics.metric_name: Health and Care Worker visas granted (main applicants)
metrics.source_name: Home Office, Immigration system statistics, year ending December 2025 — Why do people come to the UK? Work
metrics.notes: Down 51% on the previous year; dependants 35,444 (down 58%). Down from a peak of 145,823 main applicants in the year ending December 2023. The care worker route closed to new overseas recruitment in 2025.

metrics.source_name: Home Office, Immigration system statistics, year ending December 2025 — Why do people come to the UK? Family
metrics.notes: Down 22% on the previous year. Includes Partner, Child and Refugee Family Reunion (18,869) visas. Excludes work/study dependants and EU Settlement Scheme family permits.

metrics.metric_name: Total entry clearance visas granted (excluding visitor and transit)
metrics.source_name: Home Office, Immigration system statistics, year ending December 2025 — How many people come to the UK each year?
metrics.notes: Main applicants plus dependants; 15% fewer than the previous year. Breakdown: Study 429,254; Work 261,112; Family 66,610; Other 52,431. Excludes about 2.2 million Visitor and 20,108 Transit visas.

metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory (University of Oxford), Long-term international migration flows to and from the UK (citing ONS)
metrics.notes: Indian nationals were the largest single nationality for overall immigration (16%), followed by Chinese and Pakistani (about 7% each). For study visas specifically, India (95,231 main applicants, 23%) and China (89,019, 22%) lead.
